title: Module 1 quiz: The App Router model

questions.prompt: Default rendering location for components in the App Router?

choices.text: Server side. Server Components are the default — they run on the server at request time, can directly access databases/secrets/filesystem, never ship JavaScript to the client, and can be async functions. Client Components opt in via `'use client'` directive — they hydrate in the browser, can use hooks and handle events, but ship JavaScript bundles. Default to Server; reach for Client only when you need state, effects, browser APIs, or event handlers. Compose by passing Server Components as children/props of Client Components.

questions.explanation: Server is default. Client opts in via 'use client' directive.

questions.prompt: Marketing pages and the logged-in app need very different chrome. Structure?

choices.text: Use route groups: `app/(marketing)/about/page.tsx` produces `/about`; `app/(app)/dashboard/page.tsx` produces `/dashboard`. Parens in folder names organize files without affecting URLs. Each group can have its own `layout.tsx` — marketing chrome and app chrome live cleanly side by side. Lets you ship two visually-distinct experiences from one codebase, deployed together, with clean URLs.

questions.explanation: Route groups (parens) organize without affecting URLs; per-group layouts.

questions.prompt: Default caching behavior of `fetch()` in Server Components?

choices.text: Cached indefinitely (static) until rebuild or explicit revalidation. Opt out: `cache: 'no-store'` for fully dynamic, `next: { revalidate: N }` for ISR with N-second freshness, `next: { tags: ['key'] }` for tag-based invalidation via `revalidateTag('key')`. Dynamic functions (cookies(), headers(), searchParams) also force the page to dynamic rendering. Cache by default = fast and CDN-friendly; opt into dynamic only when content varies per request or user.

questions.explanation: Cached by default; opt out via cache: 'no-store' or next.revalidate / next.tags.

questions.prompt: Dashboard fetches user + posts + analytics sequentially. Total time is slow. Fix?

choices.text: Parallelize via Promise.all: `const [user, posts, analytics] = await Promise.all([fetchUser(), fetchPosts(), fetchAnalytics()])`. Total time becomes max(each) instead of sum(all). Sequential await is only correct when fetch B depends on fetch A — and even then, prefer collapsing to one query (SQL JOIN beats two HTTP round-trips). Next.js dedupes identical fetches within a single request automatically.

questions.explanation: Promise.all for independent fetches; sequential await is the trap.

questions.prompt: Best Suspense layout for a dashboard with a hero, slow chart, and slow list?

choices.text: One Suspense around the whole page.

Hero renders eagerly (no Suspense — fast and above-the-fold). Chart and list each get their own Suspense with skeleton fallback. They fetch and stream in parallel; neither blocks the other. The user sees the hero instantly, then chart and list fade in independently as each finishes loading. Page-level Suspense would block the hero too; no Suspense at all blocks the page on the slowest fetch. Granularity at the slow-component level is the win.

questions.explanation: Eager hero + per-slow-component Suspense = fast perceived load.
